one_line_takeaway: A deployment-focused SoK plus cross-model IP-classifier study showing that current LLM watermarks make outputs easy to attribute (>90% F1) but inflate perplexity by up to ~20x and can wreck downstream accuracy, so they are not yet ready for real-world use.
key_finding: Watermarks reliably improve source attribution but at a real and often severe utility cost, so the paper concludes WMs are NOT yet ready for real-world LLM deployment. Concretely: even without any WM, LLM outputs are distinguishable (IP classifiers exceed 70% F1, LSTM best at 86%); adding WMs pushes every classifier above 90% F1 (EXP or SemStamp let the OPT classifier reach 98%, and the weak T5 classifier jumps from 49% to 78%), an overall 2%-31% F1 gain. But watermarking inflates generation perplexity substantially (average 3.35 -> up to 36.85 for SemStamp, i.e. +1001.5%; OPT under SemStamp goes 3.55 -> 76.91, +2066%), while KGW is the mildest (+20.6% average). MMLU accuracy is mostly only mildly hurt (<=2.3% for KGW/EXP/Unigram/UPV) but SIR and Adaptive cause large drops (e.g. Mistral 58.9% -> 20.9% under SIR). Removal/paraphrase attacks (WM-removal, Dipper, substitution) succeed at evading detection but themselves raise perplexity 2-6x (Mistral+SemStamp 39.31 -> 241), so evasion is costly to text quality. A notable qualitative risk: numerical tokens are frequently corrupted by WMs (e.g. '2.5 percent' shifted to '1 percent' / '3.5 percent'), threatening integrity in finance/legal/scientific text.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) on watermarks (WMs) for LLMs, centered on the deployment question 'Are watermarks in LLMs ready for deployment?'. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of LLM watermarking mechanisms organized by where the WM is injected (WM during logits generation, WM during sampling process split into token-based and sentence-based, WM during LLM training split into trigger-based/global/architecture, and miscellaneous mixed/multiple-output/prompt-based methods), plus a companion split of IP checkers into green/red-list statistical-test detectors vs. pseudo-random-sequence-alignment detectors; (2) a NOVEL cross-model IP classifier that reframes IP checking as a multi-class classification problem across several LLMs simultaneously, rather than applying each WM's native single-model detector in isolation; and (3) an extensive empirical study of how existing WMs affect LLM utility and detectability under both attack-free and attack settings. Note: KGW, EXP, SIR, SemStamp, Unigram, Adaptive, UPV and the attacks (Zhang et al. WM-removal, Dipper, Substitution) are EXISTING/cited techniques the authors evaluate, not their own inventions; the authors' contribution is the taxonomy, the cross-model classifier framing, and the measurement study/conclusions.
threat_model: Model-stealing / IP-theft against a proprietary cloud-hosted LLM served via API. The adversary cannot access model weights or architecture but can query the API with N task-specific prompts, collect input-output pairs, and fine-tune a local surrogate model (theta_adv) that mimics the cloud model's behavior in a domain, then resell cheaper unlicensed services, bypass fees, or reverse-engineer vulnerabilities. Defense = the provider embeds an imperceptible watermark into LLM outputs so an IP checker can later statistically detect whether a suspicious model was trained on the provider's watermarked outputs. Two adaptive attacks against the WM defense are considered: WM-removal attacks (paraphrasing / synonym substitution / reordering, possibly with a high-quality oracle model, to strip the pattern while preserving quality) and spoofing attacks (forging text that is falsely flagged as watermarked to damage provider reputation).
limitations: Authors acknowledge that for SIR and Adaptive they used publicly released pre-trained WM/semantic-mapping models WITHOUT fine-tuning to their tasks (limited time/compute), which likely depressed those methods' MMLU results and confounds the SIR/Adaptive utility comparison. SemStamp's sentence-level sampling is inherently ill-suited to token-level MMLU, another apples-to-oranges caveat. Broader limits: evaluation is confined to four 7B open models and seven WMs (no frontier/closed commercial models actually deploying WMs); the 'IP protection' claim is measured as classifier F1 on distinguishing outputs, which is a proxy for real theft-detection rather than an end-to-end surrogate-model-stealing experiment; spoofing attacks are discussed in the taxonomy but the empirical attack study covers only removal/paraphrase attacks. The paper itself flags open gaps: no certified-robustness WMs exist, token-sensitivity and semantic drift are unquantified, and WM interaction with alignment is untested.

datasets_used: C4 benchmark: 10,000 training and 2,000 test samples, each a news-like string truncated to a 200-token prompt with the next 200 tokens as the baseline completion
MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding), 57 subjects across STEM/humanities/social sciences, used as the downstream QA utility task
WordNet (used by the substitution attack for synonym replacement)
quantitative_results: Without WM, IP classifiers achieve >70% F1 distinguishing LLM sources; LSTM best at 86%; T5 worst at 47% (49% in another setting), rising to 64% with 100,000 training samples
With WM, all classifiers exceed 90% F1; overall F1 improvement of 2%-31% (19%-31% for ALBERT, T5, Galactica, SmolLM); EXP or SemStamp let the OPT classifier reach 98%; T5 rises from 49% to 78%

Perplexity (Table 2, avg over 4 LLMs): No-WM 3.35; KGW 4.03 (+20.6%); UPV 4.59 (+37.1%); Unigram 9.65 (+188.4%); Adaptive 11.36 (+239.6%); SIR 12.92 (+286.1%); EXP 20.12 (+501.6%); SemStamp 36.85 (+1001.5%). Worst single case: OPT+SemStamp 3.55 -> 76.91 (+2066.5%)

MMLU accuracy (Table 3): most WMs cost <=2.3% (e.g. LLaMA-2 No-WM 46.7% -> KGW 44.5%); SIR and Adaptive drop sharply (Mistral 58.9% -> 20.9% under SIR; LLaMA-2 46.7% -> 27.9% under SIR; Mistral -> 37.9% under Adaptive)
Attacks evade detection (high attack success rate / low WM detection) but raise perplexity 2-6x; Mistral+SemStamp perplexity jumps from 39.31 to ~241 after WM-removal
Qualitative numerical-token corruption: original 'Nasdaq dropped 0.7 percent' altered to 0.6/1.2/1.4 percent across WMs; '2.5 percent annual rate' shifted to '1 percent' or '3.5 percent'
US LLM market cited at $781.1M by 2022 (Fig. 4) as deployment motivation
baselines_compared: LLMs watermarked/evaluated: LLaMA-2 7B, Mistral 7B, OPT 6.7B, Falcon 7B
Watermark schemes (7): KGW, EXP, SIR, SemStamp, Unigram, Adaptive, UPV
IP-classifier architectures (8): LSTM (non-transformer), ALBERT (11.8M), DeBERTa (86M), GPT-2 (125M), Galactica (125M), OPT-small (125M), SmolLM (135M), T5 (60.5M, encoder-decoder)
Attacks against WMs (3): WM-removal (Zhang et al. quality-preserving removal), Dipper paraphrase, Substitution (WordNet synonym)
